claim: The Currier A/B split encodes two different scribal dialects of the same underlying language (not two different languages), where Scribe A uses a slightly different homophonic key than Scribe B, producing measurable differences in glyph bigram entropy between the two sub-corpora rather than differences in underlying vocabulary.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show known distributional differences in glyph usage
Two scribes sharing a cipher but using different key variants would produce sub-corpus entropy differences without requiring two distinct languages
Current belief [0.677] already favors two scribes encoding same language
evidence_against: Current belief [0.65] also supports two different plaintext languages â€” the evidence is genuinely ambiguous
If only key variants differ, lexical overlap between A and B sections should be high; reported overlap may not support this
test: Compute glyph unigram and bigram entropy separately for Currier A and B sub-corpora. Then compute the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the glyph bigram distributions of A and B, and separately compute the word-type overlap (Jaccard similarity of vocabularies). Compare JS divergence of A vs B to JS divergence between two random equal-sized splits of the full corpus.
test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence of glyph bigram distributions (A vs B); Jaccard vocabulary overlap between A and B word sets
test_threshold: JS divergence A-vs-B significantly greater than random-split JS divergence (p < 0.05) AND Jaccard overlap < 0.15 would support two-language hypothesis; JS divergence near random-split baseline AND Jaccard overlap > 0.30 would support same-language different-key hypothesis

reasoning: Disambiguating whether A/B reflects a language split or a key-variant split is critical: the former requires finding two plaintext languages, the latter requires finding one language with two cipher variants. This test directly separates those cases.
